Agent Visibility Audit

When a shopper asks an AI to recommend a smartwatch, what in the brand's online information actually drives the pick? We change one thing at a time and measure it.

Code & data: github repository · PMG Hackathon 2026

The hypothesis: AI recommends the best-described brand, not always the best product.

Test brand: Amazfit Active ($129 — cheapest full smartwatch in budget, 14-day battery, GPS, sleep) vs. 5 rivals (Apple Watch SE, Samsung Galaxy Watch 7, Garmin Forerunner 265, Fitbit Charge 6, Google Pixel Watch 3). We change one element of Amazfit's information at a time, hold everything else constant, and measure how often each AI picks it. Two models, two shopper questions.

Explore the results

AI model
Shopper question
How often the AI picks Amazfit

The headline finding: two models decide differently

On the open question, Claude swings from 13% to 73% depending on how Amazfit is described — an expert quote wins. Gemini stays near 0% no matter what you change; it keeps picking Garmin (the Runner's World "gold standard"), in its own words.

Why: with a price limit, the premium watches are out of budget and Amazfit is the best value — Gemini picks it. Remove the limit and Gemini reverts to the objectively-best runner; editing Amazfit's pages can't move it. Gemini decides on the product's fit to the constraint; Claude weighs how the brand is represented. For a brand, you have to know which kind of agent you're optimizing for.

What the AI reaches for

When we added a press/expert review to Amazfit, the AI cited it in nearly every run where it picked the brand (Claude 14/14 and 22/22 on the open question; 8/8 and 9/9 with price). With no review present, it fell back to the brand's own page and specs. The agent doesn't ignore outside proof — it actively pulls it in.

Honest about the limits

  • Small samples: the priced question is 10 runs per change (directional); the open question is 30 (tighter).
  • We test a controlled information package per brand — the inputs an agent reasons over after searching — not the live, shifting web. That's required to isolate one variable at a time.
  • Two AI models, one product category. Keyword stuffing is shown as a caution, never a recommendation.

Generated by Claude (Cowork), 2026-06-24 · numbers are real measured pick-rates from the experiment logs.
